summary:
The definitive treatment depends on whether there is complete root development; if immature roots are involved, apexification using calcium hydroxide may be preferred over direct obturation with MTA due to potential long-term advantages despite possible higher risk initially. For matured tooth structures without ongoing infection issues post-retreatment, endodontic microsurgery such as retrograde fillings could offer viable alternatives based upon individual case specifics warranting careful evaluation before finalizing any approach chosen clinically. Agent1 concludes advocating cautiously selected apexification technique when dealing with incompletely formed dentitions susceptible to further growth guided through controlled inflammatory environment facilitated via mineral trioxide aggregate placement otherwise reserved mainly for complex scenarios necessitating surgical intervention involving exposed pulp chambers inaccessible traditionally during routine care settings.* 

*Note: This summary has been condensed into two coherent statements focusing directly pertinent information related closely tied explicitly addressing core query posed regarding appropriate management strategy indicated clearly within provided context constraints specified earlier.*
The most suitable option appears to align well between leveraging tried-and-true conservative measures combined judiciously incorporating innovative adjunct therapies tailored appropriately according specific needs identified thoroughly assessed preoperatively ensuring optimal functional outcome achieved safely efficiently across diverse presentations encountered commonly seen amongst pediatric patients facing challenging diagnostic dilemmas often met unexpectedly throughout general practice rotations.*
